__EMPTY_7: Malaysia's Artificial Intelligence Roadmap 2021-2025 (launched in Aug 2022 by Malaysian Ministry of Science, Technology, and Innovation (MOSTI))
__EMPTY_8:
- Malaysia's Artificial Intelligence Roadmap 2021-2025  describes how Malaysia’s AI capabilities will be harnessed, catalysed and propelled from 2021-25

- Vision: “Make Malaysia a nation where Artificial Intelligence augments JOBS, drives NATIONAL COMPETITIVENESS, encourages INNOVATION & ENTREPRENEURSHIP to bring economic prosperity, social good and improves PEOPLE’S WELL BEING”

- Mission: “To create a THRIVING NATIONAL AI ECOSYSTEM that allows EVERYONE (government, business, and people) to capitalise on the BENEFITS OF AI in a SECURED AND SAFE manner for economic prosperity and social well-being”

- Envisioned outcomes:
1. Establishing AI governance
2. Advancing AI R&D
3. Escalating digital infrastructure to enable AI
4. Fostering AI Talents
5.Acculturating AI
6. Kickstarting a national AI innovation ecosystem

- As of the third quarter of 2024, Malaysia’s National AI Roadmap 2021-2025 has achieved 63% completion. The roadmap is overseen by NAIO

__EMPTY_8:
-The National Guidelines on AI Governance and Ethics are intended to establish Responsible AI practices as the government aspires to enhance the development and deployment of AI 

- Guidelines are structured across three levels: Users, Regulators, and Developers

- Built on 7 AI Principles of fairness; reliability, safety and human control; privacy and security; inclusiveness; transparency, accountability, and human-centricity, the AIGE lays a strong foundation for future AI legislation

__EMPTY_7: 1. AI in Healthcare - establishment of the Digital Health Research and Innovation Unit
__EMPTY_8:
- The National AI Roadmap has defined 11 national AI Use cases across 5 sectors (Agriculture and forestry, healthcare, smart cities and transportation, education and public services)

-Both Federal Government and the State Governments have embarked on numerous AI initiatives.
- Examples:
a. Healthcare:  Ministry of Health unveiled “DR. MATA”, an AI-powered diagnostic tool to detect diabetic retinopathy, a leading cause of blindness among diabetics.

AI is also digitally transforming the operations of public health clinics, with the roll-out of cloud-based digital management system (CCMS), enhancing efficiency and accessibility of healthcare services. 

b. Smart cities and transportation: AI adoption is traffic management. In Kuala Lumpur, a citywide CCTV network enables AI platforms to count vehicles, classify them by type, and recognise licence plates. This allows real-time traffic analysis

c.Financial services: In August 2024, the Central Bank of Malaysia, in partnership with PayNet, and other financial institutions, launched the National Fraud Portal (NFP), utilising AI to combat financial fraud through predictive analysis. 

d. Agriculture: Malaysia launched the world’s first AI-driven smart palm oil mill

__EMPTY_5: Digital ID: Availability of National ID systems, biometric coverage, authentication mechanisms, interoperability etc
__EMPTY_7:
Present - MyDigital ID - registration begin in 2024 / MyGOV Malaysia with a budget of RM80 million

Governed by Ministry of Digital - My Digital ID Sdn Bhd
__EMPTY_8:
MyDigital ID is a government initiative for a secure and trusted digital identity solution. 

Identity verification refers directly to the government database, without storing any personal data and does not replace the MyKad system. It also facilitates access to online applications and portals using only one password.

__EMPTY_5: Digital Payments : Availability, Interoperability, financial inclusion
__EMPTY_7: Present - Real-time Retail Payments Platform (RPP) started in 2018
__EMPTY_8:
• Real-time Retail Payments Platform (RPP) is a fast payment system launched in 2018) that enables Malaysian consumers, businesses and government agencies to make real-time, data-rich payments between accounts at participating financial institutions

• RPP was launched by PayNet, the operator of shared payments infrastructure formed by merger of MEPS (Malaysian Electronic Payment System) - an interbank network service provider in Malaysia and MyClear (Malaysian Electronic Clearing Corporation Sdn Bhd) - a wholly-owned subsidiary of Bank Negara Malaysia

__EMPTY_5: Data Exchange: Availability, data sharing frameworks, consent mechanisms, interoperability standards
__EMPTY_7: Present - Malaysian Government Central Data Exchange (MyGDX)
__EMPTY_8: MyGDX is a seamless data sharing ecosystem that encompasses digital governance and a secured platform to empower and enhance end-to-end (E2E) online services towards a data-driven government. The implementation of MyGDX allows the sharing of classified data to be coordinated more efficiently and reduces the cost of infrastructure and system integration. A shared platform makes you quicker.

__EMPTY_8:
The administrative structure of the Federal Government in Malaysia is responsible for administering the entire country covering various areas such as security, finance, education, health and foreign relations.

It is led by the Federal Cabinet headed by the Prime Minister, and assisted by ministries, departments and implementing agencies under it.

Malaysia practices a Parliamentary Democracy system under the administration of a Constitutional Monarchy with His Majesty the Yang di-Pertuan Agong as Head of State. 

The Parliamentary Democracy Government System divides the power of governance into three parts:

Executive Power
Judicial Power
Legislative Power

__EMPTY_7:
Public Sector Capacity 

Malaysia has implemented various strategies and programs to develop digital competencies within the public sector . Key initiatives include the Malaysia Digital Economy Blueprint (MDEB), the Public Sector ICT Strategic Plan, and partnerships with the private sector to provide targeted digital skills training.
__EMPTY_8:
National frameworks and programs - 
1. Malaysia Digital Economy Blueprint (MyDIGITAL) - Launched in 2021- aims to transform Malaysia into a digitally-driven, high-income nation. A central component is the upskilling of public sector employees to leverage digital technologies effectively in their roles, ensuring efficient and modernized public service delivery

2.Public Sector ICT Strategic Plan (National Digital Department/Jabatan Digital Negara) - enhancing digital literacy and technical skills among government employees.

3. AI Adoption in the Public Sector (Feb 2025) -Public Sector AI Adaptation Guidelines to ensure responsible and ethical AI adoption in government agencies 

4. National Talent Framework - Data Science Digital Talent Group which focuses on Big Data Analytics and Artificial Intelligence (AI) - May 2024

5. Public-Private Partnerships - 
2024 - Microsoft committed to training 300,000 Malaysians in AI skills

6. Malaysia Digital Workforce Training Directory - directory offers a range of endorsed digital technology courses.

7. State level initiatives - 
a. The Selangor State Government - establish multiple AI centres in 2025
b. Johar - emerging as a major AI-powered data centre hub, housing 78.6% of Malaysia’s IT capacity and expected to surpass 1GW in data centre capacity.
c. Sarawak -launching an AI Centre to integrate AI into digital infrastructure and drive innovation

Gaps:No uniform mandate requiring all new public employees to undergo training in digital skills

__EMPTY_8:
Quote by
 
1. YAB Dato' Seri Anwar bin Ibrahim, PM of Malaysia
If you want to ensure that an emerging economy succeeds, remains competitive, and sustainable, then it has to be through a quantum leap. And AI is the answer for that, for the matter of digital transformation.

2. YB Gobind Singh Deo, Minister of Digital
It is imperative that we bring AI to all sectors, reaching every segment of society – from the young to the elderly in every corner of the nation. This is to ensure no one is left behind and that everyone benefits from this technological shift.

Secretary-General of MOSTI established an AI Governance Structure, consisting of a Steering Committee, a Technical Committee, and five Working Committees on Governance, Legal and Ethics (Working Group 1), Collaboration, Enabler and Innovation Hub (Working Group 2), Talent Development (Working Group 3), Research, Innovation and Data Sharing (Working Group 4) and Acculturation and Communication (Working Group 5).

The Steering Committee’s progress is monitored by the National Digital Economy and Fourth Industrial Revolution Council (MED4IRN), chaired by the Prime Minister. MED4IRN also works to harmonise state-level digital economy plans with national strategies.

__EMPTY_7: 1. Malaysian National AI Office (NAIO) - Central authority for advancing the Malaysia’s AI agenda
__EMPTY_8:
1. Malaysian National AI Office (NAIO) launched in Dec 2024

- Goal: Transforming Malaysia from an AI consumer into an AI producer by building a robust AI ecosystem that will accelerate economic growth, improve public services, and ensure inclusive and equitable access to AI where ‘No One is Left Behind’.

- Aim: Drive AI Investments, promote AI innovation, foster collaboration, formulate robust policy and ensure governance and security

- NAIO has 7 deliverables

Malaysia’s AI regulatory approach is still evolving, and future AI policies and strategies may provide specific mandatory standards as the National AI Innovation Ecosystem matures.

MOSTI working closely with Ministry of Digital are continuously assessing digital and AI-related initiatives under broader digital economy strategies to ensure that Malaysia’s policies remain aligned with technological advancements and societal needs.

__EMPTY_8:
Existing partnerships between NAIO and other companies - 

1. Toshiba - Partnering with MADA to integrate TOSHIBA’s Weather Forecast AI technology into the Smart Farming Project (Projek Ladang Padi IR4.0) in Kedah's Muda region

2. Global AI Village - Partnering with NAIO to incubate high-impact AI applications for Malaysian corporations and government in energy, cybersecurity, agriculture, supply chain, transportation, and healthcare

3. YTL Power International Bhd - Developing Ilmu 0.1, a Malaysian LLM built, owned, and operated by Malaysians in Malaysia.

4. Microsoft - Partnering with NAIO to launch a national AI Adoption Accelerator, promoting AI in key sectors like the public sector and creating an AI Governance Sandbox framework.

5. Google - Running a pilot project with the Ministry of Digital to equip 100 officers with AI-powered solutions to automate tasks, analyze data, and streamline workflows, enabling focus on higher-value work and citizen engagement.

6. Amazon Web Services - Partnering with NAIO to empower Malaysians with AI skills and infrastructure for national and global ecosystem development.

The private sector integrates AI across various industries including medical and healthcare, agriculture, transportation, education, business and finance. Companies such as Petronas, JCorp, WISE AI, and Phison are leading adopters. Industry enablers such as YTL and Iskandar Investment actively champion smart city initiatives and AI ecosystem development.

Managed by MOSTI and supported by MD, MOHE, MOE, MITI, MOC and KESUMA, 22 strategic initiatives have been implemented. These include launching a national AI governance structure, publishing AIGE guidelines, advancing AI research and commercialization, fostering talent, and building infrastructure.

The Malaysian government has identified ten key socio-economic drivers under the 10-10
Malaysian Science, Technology, Innovation, and Economy (MySTIE) Framework that
are vital for national development:
1. Energy
2. Education
3. Medical and Healthcare
4. Business and Finance Services
5. Smart Technology and Systems
6. Smart Cities and Transportation
7. Water and Food
8. Environment and Biodiversity
9. Culture, Art, and Tourism
10. Agriculture and Forestry
Across these sectors, Malaysia has identified 10 Science and Technology (S&T)
Drivers, including Advanced Intelligent Systems, of which Artificial Intelligence (AI)
is a core component

__EMPTY_7: Between 2022 and 2024, Malaysia secured over USD 18 billion in AI-related foreign direct investment (FDI) from global tech leaders such as Google, Microsoft, Nvidia, AWS etc, complemented by RM114.7 billion in data centre and cloud service investments.
__EMPTY_8:
Malaysia has attracted substantial private investments in AI technologies, underscoring the
sector’s potential. Notable investments include:
-Microsoft: USD 2.2 billion for cloud and AI services.
-Google: USD 2 billion for its first data centre and Google Cloud region.
-ByteDance: Approximately USD 2.13 billion for an AI hub.
-Oracle: Over USD 6.5 billion to establish its first public cloud region.

In terms of Government Budget Allocations for R&D (GBARD) on AI-related funding,
according to the Malaysian Ministry of Finance (MoF), the allocation of USD 114 million
in 2024 illustrates Malaysia’s commitment to advancing AI in alignment with broader R&D
expenditure goals and ambitions to become a key player in AI innovation. For example, the
government allocated RM600 million (approximately USD 141 million) for education and
R&D, which includes AI initiatives. In addition, the government allocated RM10 million (USD
2.35 million) in the 2025 Budget for the establishment of the National AI Office (NAIO),
with the goal of positioning Malaysia as a regional AI leader. Another significant investment
of RM50 million (USD 11.75 million) has been designated to expand AI-related education
across Malaysia’s research universities. These investments reflect Malaysia’s strategic
emphasis on AI infrastructure, education, and R&D. According to the Malaysian Ministry of
Finance (MoF) in the Malaysia Budget 2025, the government allocated USD 125 million. The
amount allocated to MOSTI for AI governance was RM 285 million; and for NAIO under the
Ministry of Digital is RM 10 million; for community engagement is RM 5 million and RM64.1
billion (USD 14.3 billion) for education and research initiatives

Challenges - 
Weak regulatory and institutional support
 Poor data governance and sharing
 Fragmented coordination
 Low public awareness and trust
 Limited localization and ownership
 Skills gaps and talent drain
 Infrastructure challenges

The Governance,
Regulation, and Ethics Working Committee is currently benchmarking and reviewing laws,
policies, and regulations to establish a legal framework. Key challenges include:
 Limited technical expertise and resources.
 Dependency on imported AI solutions, leading to fewer local use cases.
 The absence of a dedicated regulatory framework.
 The need to harmonize AI governance with existing sectoral laws.
